reasoning: The land border crossings between Benin and Nigeria's Kwara state were closed by Benin on May 7, 2025, in response to the active movement of a newly emerged terrorist group, "Mahmuda," which is attempting to cross into Benin due to Nigerian military pressure. Since the initial closure, Benin has escalated its clampdown by extending border closures to Lagos and waterway routes to Ogun state as late as May 21, 2025. Given that the knowledge cutoff is May 31, 2025, there is only one month left for the borders to reopen to satisfy the resolution criteria. Border closures in West Africa prompted by active militant groups or arms smuggling historically last for several months or years (e.g., Nigeria's 2019-2020 closures, Benin-Niger closures since 2023). While Nigerian legislators passed a motion on May 13 urging a diplomatic resolution, there is no evidence that the militant threat has been neutralized or that diplomatic talks have progressed sufficiently to reverse an expanding blockade. Finding a resolution in such a short window (before July 1, 2025) is therefore highly unlikely.
